backlog.name: Interp-guided reflection routing: internal states vs output confidence for where to reflect, fixed budget
backlog.definition: Reflection loop re-does at most B of N answer-steps under a FIXED budget. Does picking WHICH steps to reflect on by an INTERNAL-activation signal beat picking by OUTPUT confidence, at equal B? Run on gpt2/pythia-160m on a checkable task (2-3 digit addition) where the base model errs measurably. Rank steps by likely-wrongness: INTERNAL=linear probe on residual activations predicting step-correctness; OUTPUT=logit-margin/entropy. Reflect action=re-decode that step with a fixed corrective procedure. Budget B equal across ALL arms.
backlog.prediction: At fixed B (e.g. 20% of steps), INTERNAL-probe routing beats OUTPUT-confidence routing on final accuracy by a preregistered margin >=3 points (bootstrap CI excludes 0), and both beat RANDOM. Predicted order: oracle(reflect exactly the wrong steps) > internal-probe > output-confidence > random > no-reflection. REFUTED if internal-probe is within noise of output-confidence (internal states add no extra where-to-look signal) OR nothing beats random. POSITIVE CONTROL: the oracle arm must top the ranking, else measurement is BROKEN not refuted.
backlog.reduces_to: Hidden-state error probing (Azaria&Mitchell 2023; CCS Burns 2022) + inference-time reflection (Reflexion/self-refine). Earns keep ONLY by internal-signal BUDGET ROUTING beating output-confidence routing at EQUAL compute — an efficiency claim neither prior line makes; else refuted-as-rediscovery.

seenStress: Reflection loops spend compute uniformly; whether a model's INTERNAL states route a fixed reflection budget to the right steps better than output confidence is open and directly testable.
